title: The case system
subtitle: What cases are and how they work — an overview of all 6

hook.text: Cases are the reason Russian nouns, adjectives, and pronouns change their endings constantly. There are six of them. This might sound daunting — but cases are the most logical part of Russian grammar. Once you understand what each case does, you'll know exactly which ending to use. This lesson is the conceptual foundation. Every case lesson from here builds on it.

why.text: English uses word order to show meaning: 'the dog bites the man' vs 'the man bites the dog'. Russian uses endings instead. You can rearrange a Russian sentence almost any way you want and the meaning stays the same because the endings carry the grammatical information. Cases are what give Russian its famous word order freedom — and what makes it seem hard to learners who expect word order to do the work.

cards.title: What a case is
cards.body: A case is a set of endings that shows a noun's role in a sentence. Every noun in Russian is in one of six cases at all times. The case changes the noun's ending but not its core meaning. кот (cat) can appear as кот, кота, коту, котом, коте — same animal, different roles.

cards.title: The six cases — roles and questions
cards.body: Each case answers a question. Learning the question is the fastest way to identify which case you need.

cards.title: Case affects nouns, pronouns, and adjectives
cards.body: Every word that modifies a noun takes the same case. If the noun is in genitive, its adjective is also in genitive. If the pronoun refers to a genitive noun, it's in genitive too. This is 'agreement' — the whole phrase inflects together. You've already seen this with possessives: мой/моя/моё.

questions.prompt: How many cases does Russian have?
questions.explanation: Russian has 6 cases: Nominative, Genitive, Dative, Accusative, Instrumental, Prepositional.

questions.prompt: The nominative case answers which question?
questions.explanation: Nominative = the subject of the sentence. Кот спит — кот is nominative.

questions.prompt: In Russian, 'the dog bites the man' and 'the man bites the dog' are distinguished by:
options: Case endings — not necessarily word order
questions.answer: Case endings — not necessarily word order
questions.explanation: Cases carry meaning in Russian. Word order is flexible because endings show who does what.

cards.title: ⚠ Cases are your weak point — a direct note
cards.body: Your placement results show you have case awareness but lack drilling. This unit will not just explain cases — it will drill them systematically. Don't try to memorise all endings before you start using them. The approach that works: learn one case at a time, drill it in context, then add the next. Mixing all cases at once before any are solid creates confusion.

cards.title: Which cases matter most at A1
cards.body: Frequency at A1 level: Nominative (every sentence), Accusative (direct objects, very common), Genitive (possession, negation, quantity — extremely common), Prepositional (location — immediately useful). Dative and Instrumental come next. Learn them in this order — the first four are in almost every sentence you'll encounter.

examples.cyrillic: Nom. > Acc. > Gen. > Prep. > Dat. > Instr.
examples.meaning: Learn this order — it reflects real frequency

questions.prompt: Which case is used for the subject of a sentence?
questions.explanation: Nominative = subject. Answers 'who/what is doing the action?'

questions.prompt: Which case is used for the direct object?
questions.explanation: Accusative = direct object. Answers 'whom/what is the action directed at?'

questions.prompt: Which case is used only with prepositions — location and topic?
questions.explanation: Prepositional case is always used with a preposition (в, на, о, etc.) — never standalone.

questions.prompt: What does 'case' mean in Russian grammar?
options: A set of endings showing a noun's role in a sentence
questions.answer: A set of endings showing a noun's role in a sentence
questions.explanation: Cases are grammatical categories — endings that show whether a noun is the subject, object, possessor, etc.
Dative = indirect object. Answers 'to whom?' or 'for whom?'

questions.prompt: Which case answers 'with whom / by means of what?'
questions.explanation: Instrumental = instrument, means, accompaniment. Answers 'with whom?' or 'by means of what?'

rules: Russian has 6 cases: Nominative, Genitive, Dative, Accusative, Instrumental, Prepositional
Cases are endings that show a noun's role — not just word order
Case affects nouns, pronouns, AND adjectives — the whole phrase agrees
Priority order: Nominative > Accusative > Genitive > Prepositional > Dative > Instrumental
Prepositional is always used with a preposition — never standalone
summary.nextLesson: Next: Nominative and Accusative — the subject and direct object, the two cases you'll use in every sentence.

questions.prompt: How many cases does Russian have?
Which case is used for the subject?
Which case is the direct object case?
Which case is always used with a preposition?
What does 'case' do to a noun?
options: Changes its ending to show its role in the sentence
questions.answer: Changes its ending to show its role in the sentence
